mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes GLC 300d engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned OM654 engine typically costs £5,500–£8,500 supply only, with X254 MHEV units slightly higher. A used engine costs on average £2,500–£4,500. Fitting adds £1,400–£2,000. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your GLC.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes GLC 300d (OM654) engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM654 is a sophisticated and powerful four‑cylinder diesel, but it has several known issues. The EGR cooler vacuum diaphragm is a common failure point causing loss of power. Timing chain tensioner wear causes cold‑start rattle — and the chain is at the back of the engine, making replacement an engine‑out job. AdBlue system failures are also common. With regular maintenance and early intervention, it can be a reliable engine.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the GLC 300d engine?

acceptedAnswer.text: EGR cooler vacuum diaphragm failure (loss of power, underboost, P0299), timing chain tensioner wear (cold‑start rattle, engine‑out repair), AdBlue system failures (NOx sensors, heaters, SCR), rocker bearing wear (ticking/popping, can lead to cam scoring), oil dilution (diesel in oil), and cooling system issues.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a GLC 300d engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value and the nature of the failure. A GLC 300d is a £15,000–£35,000+ vehicle — a £6,000–£7,000 recon is 17–47% of the car's value, making replacement viable in most cases. For a timing chain failure requiring an engine‑out job (up to £3,500+), replacement may still be worthwhile. Evaluate carefully based on your car's condition and the specific repair needed.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes GLC 300d engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. The GLC 300d engine code is OM654 — a 2.0‑litre four‑cylinder turbo‑diesel. Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I prevent the common failures on a GLC 300d?

acceptedAnswer.text: On the EGR cooler: if you experience loss of power or engine warning light, have the EGR system inspected immediately. On the timing chain: regular oil changes with Mercedes‑approved MB 229.51/229.52 oil and shortened intervals (5,000–8,000 miles) are essential. On the AdBlue system: use high‑quality AdBlue from reputable sources and have the system serviced regularly. At the first sign of cold‑start rattle, loss of power or AdBlue warnings, book an inspection immediately.

replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the current OM654 generation. A replacement OM654 engine costs on average £4,500–£6,000 for a used unit, with reconditioned units significantly higher.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a GLC 300d engine typically takes 14–18 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer). The OM654’s timing chain is at the back of the engine, making many repairs engine‑out jobs.

problems.title: EGR Cooler Vacuum Diaphragm Failure (OM654 — All Years)
problems.symptoms: Loss of power, underboost (P0299 code), engine warning light, rough running, white smoke from exhaust, coolant loss.
problems.repairCost: £600–£1,500 (EGR cooler replacement, vacuum diaphragm)【PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet】
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (if caught before overheating)

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if you experience loss of power or engine warning light

problems.recommendation: The OM654’s EGR cooler vacuum diaphragm is a common failure point. The diaphragm can fail repeatedly, causing vacuum leaks, underboost and loss of power. If you experience loss of power or an engine warning light, have the EGR system inspected. Carbon buildup and corrosion are also common causes of EGR cooler failure. Catching it early prevents more serious engine damage.

problems.title: Timing Chain Tensioner Wear (OM654 — All Years)
problems.symptoms: Rattling noise on cold start (lasting a few seconds), metallic ticking, engine warning light with timing‑related fault codes.
problems.repairCost: £1,500–£3,500 (tensioners, chain guides and chain replacement)【PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet】

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear rattle, book an inspection today

problems.recommendation: The OM654’s timing chain is mounted at the back of the engine, making replacement an engine‑out job. If the chain snaps, there is a risk of sudden loss of propulsion and severe engine damage. Regular oil changes with Mercedes‑approved oil (MB 229.51/229.52) are essential. At the first sign of cold‑start rattle, have the timing chain system inspected immediately.

problems.title: AdBlue System Failures (OM654 — All Years)
problems.symptoms: Check engine light, AdBlue warning, reduced power, countdown to start restriction (usually 500 miles), engine warning light.
problems.repairCost: £500–£2,500 (sensor replacement, AdBlue heater, SCR system repair)【PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet】

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if AdBlue countdown appears

problems.recommendation: The AdBlue (SCR) system on the OM654 is a common failure point. Faults include NOx sensors, level sensors, heaters and the dosing unit. If the AdBlue system fails and the countdown reaches zero, the car will not start. Use high‑quality AdBlue from reputable sources and have the system serviced regularly. At the first sign of an AdBlue warning, book an inspection immediately.

rows.whenItMakesSense: Replace diaphragm or cooler — catch it early
Engine‑out job — catch it early before chain snaps
Sensor or heater replacement — use quality AdBlue
Catch it early — before cam lobes are scored
Little left to salvage — replacement is the only option

buyingChecks.title: Buying This GLC 300d? Check These First
buyIf: * No cold‑start rattle — timing chain tensioner in good condition
* No loss of power or engine warning light — EGR cooler in good condition
* AdBlue system serviced — quality AdBlue used
* Full Mercedes service history with MB‑spec oil (MB 229.51/229.52)
avoidIf: * Cold‑start rattle with no tensioner history — timing chain may be failing — walk away
* Loss of power with no diagnosis — EGR cooler may be failing
* AdBlue warning light with no diagnosis — potential SCR system failure
* Poor service history — OM654 engines are sensitive to neglect

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The OM654 is Mercedes’ most advanced four‑cylinder diesel — smooth, powerful and efficient. But it has several well‑documented weaknesses: the EGR cooler vacuum diaphragm (loss of power), timing chain tensioner wear (cold‑start rattle — engine‑out job), rocker bearing wear and AdBlue system failures. Buy on full service history, listen for cold‑start rattle and check for AdBlue warnings. A well‑maintained GLC 300d is a rewarding, long‑lived SUV; a neglected one is a £7,000+ bill waiting to happen.

cta.label: → Compare GLC 300d engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes GLC 300d (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for rattling; timing chain tensioner indicator
* Loss of Power — Check for underboost; EGR cooler vacuum diaphragm indicator
* AdBlue System — Check for warning lights — AdBlue system failure indicator
* Service History — including engine era (OM654) and confirmed oil‑change intervals (MB 229.51/229.52)
* Oil Level — Check for over‑ or under‑filling; oil dilution is a known concern
* Coolant Level — Check for unexplained loss; EGR cooler or cooling system issues
* Turbo Health — Listen for whistling or whining; turbo actuator issues possible
* MOT History — Check for emissions‑related failures (AdBlue/SCR)
